Grounds the deadline in plausible IT reality (rolling retention + cutover image), and the 'why no preservation order yet' answer is in-world: death still ruled natural, no homicide number, on-call judge out till Tuesday — so Mara must first FLIP the manner of death (Gate 2) to even subpoena.

act_structure: PROLOGUE / ACT 1 — 'The Quiet Hours' (Friday evening): A natural death that won't sit right. Mara visits Daniel, learns Agnes is dead, walks Room 114, meets the cast, and teaches the player the Case Board. The hook: the call-light/badge logs show nobody came for 47 minutes. LEAD GATE: push Dr. Idris to order a full tox screen and pull the CGM data — converting 'natural' into 'survivable, and nobody came.'

ACT 2A — 'Engineered Absence' (Saturday, racing the walkthrough): With survivability proven, the player reconstructs the swap — East forced GREEN, Petrak's real event inflated to AMBER-FALL, every aide (and the float) swept West. Clears Yvette (her memos), exposes the fabricated-inflation. Establishes this was a HAND, not the algorithm. LEAD GATE: assemble enough to get Dr. Idris to reclassify manner of death NATURAL → UNDETERMINED, opening a case number.

ACT 2B — 'The Hand in the Machine' (Saturday night, the cutover looming): The reclassification unlocks a preservation order/subpoena. The player chases the SESSION metadata — discovers the CADENCE_SVC switch, the workstation password reset, and the door-path correlation tying the anonymous session to Bernard's body. Discharges Owen (alibi+reframed motive) and probes Diane (data room). LEAD GATE: preserve the door-session correlation table BEFORE the Saturday-night DB imaging, or lose the linchpin (branch).

ACT 3 — 'Two Hands' (Sunday → Monday 9am): The inversion lands — Agnes's voice memos + notebook + the overheard plea reveal she was trying to SAVE Bernard; Rosa's reassignment and the muted CGM alarm support 'reckless, not premeditated.' Bernard's interview cracks under the timeline and his own draft text. Diane's bonus + contingency memo + SMS expose the incentive and the scapegoat plan. FINAL ACCUSATION: name Bernard's crime correctly (reckless homicide / depraved indifference, NOT premeditated murder) AND decide the Diane fork (charge her separately for the fraud-incentive + reckless endangerment, or wrongly try to pin the death on her). Live-stakes coda: forcing an emergency staffing floor that protects Daniel's wing for the very night the machine was primed to repeat.
act1_beat_sheet: COLD OPEN (cutscene, ~90s, no UI): Glenharrow, Wednesday night, from Agnes's POV. We HEAR her record a voice memo — warm, tired, ICU-brisk: 'Note to self. Talked to Bernie again. Giving him till Monday to come in with me — the right way. He's a good boy buried under a bad year.' She does the crossword. The shift board in the hall flicks East from AMBER to GREEN. Her glucose monitor buzzes once — and her hand reaches up and silences it ('Sorry, Marjorie' — to her sleeping roommate). She reaches for the pull cord. The cord light blinks at an empty nurses' station. Cut to black on the call-light's soft, unanswered chime. PURPOSE: meet Agnes alive; plant the muted alarm, the Monday deadline, the empty station — fairly, before any deduction.

BEAT 1 — Arrival (teaches LOCATIONS + personal stake): Friday dusk. Mara badges in to visit Daniel. Tutorial: examine the corridor, the glassed nurses' station, the GREEN/AMBER/RED shift board. Daniel, in a lucid window, sets two coffees and says 'Agnes is late for the crossword.' He doesn't know she's gone. Mara learns from a passing aide that Agnes 'passed peacefully Wednesday, in her sleep, bless her.' The hook lands in Mara's gut, not exposition. Teaches: tap to examine, read forensic notes, the Case Board opens.

BEAT 2 — Room 114 (teaches EVIDENCE examination): Mara talks her way into the not-yet-cleared room. Player examines each item — pull cord, insulin pen, UNOPENED glucose tablets within reach, CGM clip, oxygen concentrator, the half-finished crossword (Daniel's handwriting beside hers). Forensic notes seed the core question: glucose tablets untouched and reachable → 'a treatable crisis; so why didn't she treat it, and why did no one come?' Teaches: evidence locker, notes, and 'flag a contradiction' (the first deduction-board card: 'survivable crisis vs. no response').

BEAT 3 — The first anomaly (teaches the DEDUCTION BOARD): At the nurses' station the player pulls the basic call-light log and the prior night's badge readouts (publicly accessible facility records). The numbers: call-light fired 11:58, auto-cleared 12:39 — 47 minutes, zero badge-ins. Tutorial deduction: connect 'call-light unanswered 47 min' + 'survivable crisis' → conclusion card 'Nobody came while she was dying.' The board lights the link. Teaches: drag-to-connect clues into a conclusion.

BEAT 4 — Meet the People (teaches INTERVIEWS + branching + secrets): Short, sharp first-pass interviews that establish the roster and plant herrings honestly. YVETTE (defensive, bitter about staffing — 'you want to blame an aide? Get in line'). DR. IDRIS (smooth, deflecting — 'a frail diabetic, 71, it happens; the certificate's signed'). A glimpse of DIANE on a video call about the 'walkthrough.' BERNARD — grief-stricken, says he found her, voice cracking ('I do this job to keep people from dying alone'). Teaches: dialogue choices, pressing vs. building rapport, the Partner's notebook auto-logging 'suspicions' (in 2-player, Partner can free-roam during these).

BEAT 5 — The wall (raises stakes + introduces the clock): Mara asks for the real CareCadence logs and is stonewalled: it's a natural death, no case number, and the system migrates to Meridian's servers Monday. She sees, on the shift board, that Daniel's MEMORY-CARE wing is flagged 'hold green' for the weekend walkthrough — the exact pattern. The dread becomes personal and live. Establishes: the 48-hour clock and why she can't just subpoena yet.

BEAT 6 — LEAD-ONLY DECISION POINT (Act 1 gate): The Lead must choose how to crack 'natural.' Option A: lean on Dr. Idris for a FULL TOX SCREEN. Option B: get her to pull the CGM DEVICE DATA. (Best play, surfaced by Partner advice in 2-player: push for BOTH.) Mara confronts Idris with the untouched glucose tablets and the unanswered cord; Idris, ashamed, relents. PURPOSE: a clean LEAD-binding choice that teaches the gating mechanic and the Lead/Partner split — the Partner can advise and has seen Agnes's voice memo, but only the Lead acts.

BEAT 7 — ACT 1 BUTTON (the reframe ignites): Results return. Tox screen: CLEAN — poison and mercy-killing die on screen. CGM annotation: 'gradual, reversible decline; preventable with timely oral glucose.' The conclusion card rewrites itself from 'natural death' to ENGINEERED ABSENCE? The board surfaces a new, locked question: 'If she was savable and no one came — did the schedule fail, or did someone fail it on purpose?' Final image: Mara, alone in the dark corridor, watches the shift board tick Daniel's wing to GREEN — and we cut to Act 2. Hook secured: the murder weapon is the schedule, and it's still running tonight.
spine.twist_and_fairness: There are two stacked reframes, each fully player-derivable. REFRAME ONE (end Act 1 → Act 2): not a natural death but ENGINEERED ABSENCE. Provable: clean tox + CGM 'gradual, preventable' annotation (survivability SHOWN, not assumed — fixes the medical-determinism hole) + call-light unanswered 47 min + East forced GREEN at 11:53 + Petrak's REAL event inflated to AMBER-FALL in the same session + all three aides (incl. float Rosa) swept West. REFRAME TWO (Act 3, the steeper one): the hand was a panicked man hiding fraud, and — the inversion that dodges the worn 'whistleblower silenced' template — Agnes wasn't threatening Bernard; she was trying to SAVE him from being Diane's scapegoat, so he killed his only ally. Provable: voice memos ('giving Bernie till Monday to come in WITH me'), the overheard plea ('come in with me, Bernie, before they hang it on you'), the notebook entry recontextualized, Diane's contingency memo pre-framing Bernard as 'isolated operator error.' FAIRNESS, point by point: (1) Identity is EARNED, not read off a field — the death-night override runs under the dormant CADENCE_SVC account while months of fraud ran under Bernard's real ID; the SWITCH plus the workstation password-reset provenance plus the door-path correlation tying the anonymous session to Bernard's physical movements is the inference (fixes self-incrimination + 'logs are spoofable'). (2) The West event is inflated-not-invented (Petrak real but trivial, Yvette's 11:56 memo proves it), so the deduction is subtler and doesn't rely on absence-of-record (fixes phantom-event hole). (3) 'Reckless, not premeditated' is evidence-supported: Rosa's reassignment record shows Bernard's assumed safety net, and the CGM app log shows Agnes muted her own alarm — both proving his false belief that she wouldn't be truly alone (fixes motive-vs-outcome). (4) The Diane fork is fairly deducible: bonus doc + 'keep the board green for the walkthrough' SMS tie her to THIS push, while the contingency memo proves the scapegoat plan — yet nothing she wrote authored the override, so 'charge both for different crimes' is supported and 'pin the death on Diane' is provably wrong. Every red herring dies to a specific accessible clue: Yvette (badge trail + memos), Owen (ANPR/toll + hotel folio + 'let me get you out' texts), Diane-as-killer (off-site + audit metadata). Surprising yet inevitable: the player can reach the full truth, and the final memo of Agnes's own slurring voice makes the inevitable land emotionally, not just logically.
spine.ticking_clock: A grounded 48 hours, Friday evening to Monday 9am. Meridian signs the purchase Monday at 9am and immediately migrates CareCadence to its own stack. The single linchpin — the table that correlates door-badge sessions to audit-log overrides (what ties the anonymous CADENCE_SVC session to Bernard's body) — is a DERIVED table regenerated nightly with a rolling 7-day retention; the death-night correlation is on its last retained day and gets rebuilt/overwritten when IT images the database for the buyer's Saturday-morning walkthrough. So the real cliff is Saturday night, not Monday. Crucially, Mara can't just subpoena on Friday: the death is still ruled NATURAL (no homicide case number), the on-call judge for emergency orders is out until Tuesday, and facility counsel will only honor a signed order — so she must first FLIP the manner of death to UNDETERMINED (an Act 2A gate via Dr. Idris) before she can legally preserve anything (fixes the legally-shaky purge). Layered pressure: Diane's 'keep the board green for the walkthrough' order means Daniel's memory-care wing is primed to run dark this very weekend, so the clock isn't only about evidence vanishing — it's about the machine being staged to kill again before Monday. If the Lead mistimes the preservation step (Hardcore), the correlation table is overwritten and the case degrades to provable-fraud-but-unprovable-hand: a darker, fair ending.
spine.twoplayer_integration: The theme IS the mechanic. This is a story about a system where many inputs feed a single human who alone holds the binding action and the accountability — exactly the Lead/Partner split, and the case interrogates it directly. Both players free-browse all evidence, people, and locations. The PARTNER literally inherits Agnes's response-time NOTEBOOK as an in-UI instrument and keeps a running 'notebook of suspicions,' becoming the spiritual successor to the murdered whistleblower — observing the anomalies (the CADENCE_SVC switch, the inflated West event, Agnes's voice memos) but, like Agnes, UNABLE TO ACT. Only the LEAD commits the manner-of-death push, the preservation order, and the accusation. The asymmetry has moral teeth designed into the content: Agnes's most damning voice memo and the muted-alarm detail are easiest to find on her phone, which the Partner tends to mine while the Lead chases the audit log — so the Partner must ADVISE the Lead, dramatizing the case's thesis that 'observation without authority changed nothing for Agnes.' The whole arc rhymes: Agnes saw everything and could only beg Bernard to act; the Partner sees everything and can only advise the Lead. The FINAL FORK — charge Bernard alone (the hand) vs. Bernard AND Diane (the hand and the incentive), and getting Bernard's CHARGE right (reckless homicide, not murder) — is a genuine two-minds debate the table will argue, because the evidence genuinely supports a moral judgment, not a lookup. In single-player, the Partner is an AI advisor whose notebook hints are difficulty-scaled.
spine.difficulty_integration: CASUAL ('Family Detective'): The deduction board auto-suggests valid links and greys-out dead ends; the Partner advisor proactively flags the CADENCE_SVC anomaly, the muted-alarm clue, and Agnes's voice memo; interviews highlight contradiction-triggering lines. The Act gates accept reasonable partial reasoning. The final accusation succeeds if the player names Bernard as the hand; getting the Diane fork and the exact charge wrong yields a 'partial justice' ending with a gentle explanatory epilogue rather than a fail. DETECTIVE (default): No auto-links — the player must connect clues themselves and must affirmatively DISCHARGE all three red herrings (Yvette, Owen, Diane-as-killer) before accusing. Interviews require choosing the right pressure with the right evidence in hand. The accusation must name Bernard AND frame the charge as reckless/depraved-indifference (premeditated-murder framing is marked 'overreach — the evidence shows he expected coverage'); the Diane fork must be resolved with at least the bonus doc + SMS to charge her. HARDCORE ('Whistleblower'): No metadata highlighting — the player must independently notice the ID switch, derive the door-path correlation, and prove the West event was INFLATED (compare logged AMBER-FALL acuity against Yvette's memo and Petrak's actual condition) rather than invented. The evidence-preservation step is live and mistimable: choose the wrong sequence at the Saturday-night cliff and the correlation table is lost, forking to a sober 'fraud proven, hand unprovable' ending. The accusation is strict on BOTH the precise charge for Bernard and a fully-supported Diane fork (bonus + SMS + contingency memo), and on NOT convicting Diane of the homicide; any unsupported leap is rejected with a defense-attorney rebuttal that names the exact missing proof. Across all tiers, hints never invent facts — they only surface clues that already exist in the locker, preserving fair play.
spine.theme: Accountability versus automation, and the difference between the hand and the machine that aims it. The story indicts the real present-day practice of rationing human care with predictive labor software — not by claiming the algorithm is evil, but by showing how an institution can hide a human choice inside an automated one and how an incentive can make a person expendable and pre-select someone to blame. Its deepest beat is moral, not technological: Agnes could SEE the harm coming and had only the power to beg; the only person who could have acted chose fear; and a corporation engineered a world where the person with the binding power was the most desperate and most disposable. 'Observation without authority changed nothing for Agnes' is both the Lead/Partner mechanic and the thesis — the question of WHO is allowed to decide is, literally, the difference between life and death. The resolution refuses easy villainy: it asks the player to grieve a tragic perpetrator, convict a deniable architect for the right crime, and recognize that the machine did exactly what it was built to do.
spine.remaining_weaknesses: (1) The convergence of Agnes's deadline, her crash, and the buyout window still asks for some suspension of disbelief; it's now motivated (Bernard panics BECAUSE the walkthrough deadline collides with her Monday deadline, so the timing is causal rather than coincidental), but a skeptic may still find the collision tidy. (2) The CADENCE_SVC + door-path-correlation deduction is sturdier and earned, but it remains technical; weak in-game surfacing of the metadata UI could still leave less-attentive players stuck — mitigated by difficulty hints but reliant on strong evidence-locker design. (3) Replayability is still single-solution at its core; the added value is the moral fork (Diane charge / exact Bernard charge) and the Hardcore evidence-preservation branch (good vs. partial-justice endings), which give replay variation in OUTCOME and framing more than in WHODUNIT — players who know the culprit have less surprise on a second run. (4) The private-equity/eldercare indictment is sharp and could still tip toward sermon if the writing over-explains; it must be carried by character (Bernard's son, Daniel's crossword, Agnes's voice) rather than speeches. (5) 'Reckless vs. murder' is now evidence-supported, but some players will still emotionally read 'stripped a known-fragile diabetic of all help' as murder — this is arguably a feature (it fuels the table debate) but means the 'correct' charge framing on Detective/Hardcore may feel contestable to a minority of players, which the epilogue should acknowledge rather than paper over.

spine.title: THE LAST CLEAN TAKE
spine.logline: A celebrated audiobook narrator is found dead at her home studio the night before her biggest release — but the deeper Detective Inspector Mara Quinn digs, the more she realizes the "new" recordings playing at the memorial were made in a room that no longer exists, and the question stops being who killed Della Voss and becomes when she actually died, who has been wearing her voice for eleven weeks, and which of them stood four metres away with headphones on while she slipped under.
spine.logline_note: whodunit inverts to whendunit; the body is not staged-warm but staged-quiet, and the real crime is the theft of a death.
spine.world: Present-day rainy mid-size coastal city (Portland/Bristol register). Hub: "Saltwire Audio," a converted Victorian row-house boutique audiobook studio — a padded vocal booth with a hanging mic and pop filter, a control room with two monitors and a wall of labeled hard drives, a kitchenette, and a damp basement archive of DAT tapes and master drives. Photographable real props throughout: foam acoustic tiles, a coffee thermos, contract folders, a smart insulin pen, a label-printer, a parking-garage ticket, a hospice/memory-care brochure, a smartwatch, a continuous-glucose-monitor patch, a portable field recorder, a chest-freezer handle, a moving dolly, a hardware-store receipt. Secondary locations: Aldercrest Publishing's glass-walled downtown office; the Brightmoor long-term memory-care facility (Della's mother); and a climate-controlled self-storage unit rented in a dead man's name. Everything depictable with real stock photography and realistic human portraits; the only "tech" is plausible near-present voice-cloning, smart insulin pens, and cloud-synced CGM — all real 2024-2026 hardware.
spine.protagonist_stake: Detective Inspector Mara Quinn is partially deaf in her right ear from a line-of-duty gas explosion and wears a hearing aid she keeps hidden under her hair; she has spent her whole comeback proving she can still "hear" a case better than anyone, and is privately terrified the day her ear lies to her she'll never know. The victim, Della Voss, narrated the eighteen months of audiobooks Mara listened to through her injury rehab — Della's voice is literally the sound Mara associates with relearning to live, to sleep, to be a person again. Within hours of arriving at the memorial, Mara hears the "new" Della and something in it is wrong: too even, too clean, the breaths landing like a metronome. It is a judgment only her trained, asymmetric, ashamed ear could make — and she cannot tell if she is right or if her damaged side is finally betraying her. To solve this case she must trust the exact faculty she is most insecure about, and the voice she must prove is fake is the one that taught her she was still alive.
spine.full_truth: Della Voss died ELEVEN WEEKS before her body was "discovered." She died alone and accidentally: a Type-1 diabetic, she skipped dinner during a marathon late-night recording session, dosed her usual basal insulin timed to a food delivery she never made it to the door to collect, and slid into a slow hypoglycemic coma over roughly five hours in the booth. There was no murder. But her business partner and engineer, Theo Marsh, was in the control room that night with her talkback mic open — and the session autosave preserves ninety seconds of Della slurring "Theo... I can't... the light's wrong" while Theo, deep in a take with headphones on, answered "...you good? give me five," and went back to work. By the time he understood, she was gone. He could have reached her in twenty seconds. That failure — not any crime — is the wound that drives everything. Theo found her dead the next morning. Saltwire was insolvent (the advance had been spent keeping the studio alive and paying Della's mother's memory-care), but the real horror was the contract: Della had personally fought for a "Voice Continuation Rider" — if she delivered all contracted titles ALIVE, the synthetic-licensing rights and a sunset/retirement clause stayed with her estate (her explicit, written wish: my voice dies when I do). If she died before delivery was complete and accepted, the rights REVERTED IN PERPETUITY to Aldercrest, who could mint an infinite synthetic "Della" forever, against that wish. So Theo concealed the death and used a high-quality model trained on decades of her masters to FINISH the contracted books — splicing synthetic narration with archival outtakes, forging her phone check-ins — a doomed attempt to honor her wish by keeping her voice out of Aldercrest's perpetual machine (and to keep the money flowing to her mother). Unable to bury or face her, he let her agent, Priya Anand — who co-signed the fraud and protected her commission and reputation — improvise the unthinkable: Priya rented a freezer in Theo's late father's storage unit and froze the body to buy time. Eleven weeks later, Aldercrest's acquisitions director Gareth Vale — who privately suspected the recordings were synthetic and wanted the reversion — announced a surprise "memorial-premiere" event in 36 hours that would force either a living Della to appear or a death certificate to be filed. The conspirators could not produce a living Della. So their plan was NOT to stage a fresh homicide (that would invite the very autopsy that destroys them) — it was to deliver the final manuscript file, get it ACCEPTED, and THEN have Della "discovered" dead at home of natural causes, timestamped hours after delivery, betting that a chronically ill woman found peacefully in her own booth would get a quiet death certificate with no full autopsy and the rights would stay clear of Aldercrest. Priya thawed and placed the body the night before discovery. Their fatal miscalculation: the one detective in the city who knew Della's voice by heart refused to let it be quiet, forced a death investigation, and the autopsy caught the freeze. No one killed Della. Two people stole eleven weeks of her death — and one of them was four metres away while she died.

suspect_dossiers.name: Theo Marsh
suspect_dossiers.role: Audio engineer and co-owner of Saltwire Audio; Della's partner of fifteen years (professional, and unspoken).
suspect_dossiers.motive: To honor Della's written wish that her voice retire at her death by keeping the perpetual licensing rights out of Aldercrest's hands, and to keep money flowing to her mother's memory-care; beneath both, unbearable guilt that he heard her dying over the talkback and did not move in time.
suspect_dossiers.means: Total mastery of Della's archived masters and the voice-model tooling; physical control of the studio, her hard drives, and her phone; the engineer's ability to forge timestamps, room-tone, and check-ins.
suspect_dossiers.secret: He is the architect of the eleven-week fraud and voice-puppetry — but he did NOT cause her death; the session autosave proves he HEARD her slurring and went back to his take. His crime is concealment and fraud; his sin is the twenty seconds he didn't cross. He resisted the freezing and the final placement, and only Priya's biometrics are on the freezer. He is the emotional payload of the case.
suspect_dossiers.red_herring: He controlled every timestamp, found the body alone, had the means to fake everything, and lies fluently about Della being 'on retreat' — he looks exactly like a man who killed her and is covering it. The talkback recording first reads as proof he was present at the murder; only the CGM curve and autopsy reframe it as a death he failed to stop, not one he caused.

suspect_dossiers.name: Priya Anand
suspect_dossiers.role: Della's literary and voice agent; negotiated the Aldercrest deal.
suspect_dossiers.motive: To protect her commission and reputation from the catastrophic reversion she had warned Della about — recast by herself as 'protecting Della's wish' — and a buried, genuine grief: she understands voices better than anyone.
suspect_dossiers.means: Legal sophistication to exploit the delivery-vs-death timing; a car; the nerve to do the storage-unit retrieval and the final placement; relationships at Aldercrest.
suspect_dossiers.secret: She is the one who froze the body and who thawed and staged it the night before discovery so the death would post-date delivery. Her prints alone are on the chest-freezer handle and the moving dolly; a hardware-store receipt in her name buys the freezer and dolly straps three days after the true death; her car is on the storage-unit toll cameras that night. She turned a grief-driven cover-up into a cold operation — yet she keeps her own dead father's voice on a reel she has never been able to erase, and knew exactly what she was stealing.
suspect_dossiers.red_herring: She is the 'closest thing to a villain' and the script leans into reading her as the mastermind who orchestrated everything — but she is NOT the architect of the voice-fraud (that's Theo) and NOT the cause of death (no one is). Her cold legal evasions misdirect toward conspiracy-to-murder; the physical split proves her actual crime is desecration and obstruction.

suspect_dossiers.name: Gareth Vale
suspect_dossiers.role: Acquisitions director at Aldercrest Publishing.
suspect_dossiers.motive: Career pressure to deliver a blockbuster, plus the perpetual synthetic-'Della' rights worth a fortune if the reversion triggers.
suspect_dossiers.means: Corporate and contractual leverage; the power to schedule the memorial-premiere event that detonated the cover-up.
suspect_dossiers.secret: He privately SUSPECTED the recordings were synthetic and the narrator already dead — and said nothing, because triggering the reversion would hand HIS company a perpetual voice-puppet. He scheduled the surprise event precisely to force the issue, knowing it would resolve in his favor either way. He committed no physical act; he let a crime ripen for profit.
suspect_dossiers.red_herring: He stands to gain the most in money, pushed the event that 'killed' the timeline, and behaves evasively about what he knew — he looks like a corporate murderer-by-proxy. But his alibi is airtight (out of the country at a book fair, verifiable) and no act is his; he is a fair red herring whose self-interest mimics guilt, and the Lead can name his moral culpability on the record without it being a charge.

suspect_dossiers.name: Junie Okafor
suspect_dossiers.role: Junior narrator and Della's protégé.
suspect_dossiers.motive: Resented being passed over for the marquee titles; ambition that reads as grudge.
suspect_dossiers.means: Studio access, vocal skill, odd-hours keycard logs that look suspicious.
suspect_dossiers.secret: Theo told her the 'pickup' lines she recorded were patches for a different project; her voice innocently appears in a few seams of the synthetic tracks, making her look complicit. She is in fact the key witness: she is the first to tell Mara the breaths in the 'new' books sound inhuman, opening the entire audio thread.
suspect_dossiers.red_herring: Her voice literally IS in the fakes and her keycard puts her in the studio at strange hours — damning on paper. But she was used, not complicit; her tip is the case's first real crack, making her simultaneously the most suspicious-looking and the most useful person in the building.

suspect_dossiers.name: Della Voss
suspect_dossiers.role: The 'victim.'
suspect_dossiers.secret: Not a murder victim at all. She died alone and accidentally of a diabetic emergency in the booth she loved. The deepest reframe: the crime against Della is not her death but the eleven weeks her death was stolen — her voice puppeted against her stated wish, her body frozen and warehoused, her dying breath captured as a 'take.' It is a violation of the person after the fact, which the law strains to name and which Mara must learn to.
suspect_dossiers.red_herring: Initially the entire case is built around her as a fresh homicide victim; every grieving gesture and every 'new release' is evidence of love. The reframe re-reads all of it as fraud maintenance — the most unsettling reveal is that there was never a victim of the kind everyone assumed, only a woman whose ending was edited.

clue_map.clue: Medical examiner's field note: lividity fixed in a pattern inconsistent with the body's posture in the booth; core/tissue readings flagged 'post-mortem interval inconsistent with apparent scene.'
clue_map.source: Location: the vocal booth (scene exam) + Evidence: ME field note
clue_map.reveals: First crack: the body was placed, not died, where it lies — the death is not as recent as the scene pretends.

clue_map.clue: Autopsy report: intracellular ice-crystal damage to soft tissue (freeze-thaw artifact); decomposition arrested then resumed.
clue_map.source: Evidence: autopsy report
clue_map.reveals: The body was frozen and later thawed — the death is OLD and the scene was staged quietly, not killed there hours ago.

clue_map.clue: Autopsy report, second section: NO trauma, NO ligature marks, NO petechiae, clean toxicology, no injection sites beyond her own logged sites.
clue_map.source: Evidence: autopsy report (examine fully)
clue_map.reveals: Discrete rule-OUT of murder — there is no homicide here; whatever happened, no one struck, choked, or poisoned her.

clue_map.clue: Junie's interview: 'The breaths in the new books are wrong — nobody breathes that evenly reading aloud.'
clue_map.source: People: Junie Okafor interview
clue_map.reveals: Directs the player to audio forensics; opens the synthetic-detection thread.

clue_map.clue: Waveform of a 'latest' recording: breath intakes spaced metronomically regular; visible on the spectrogram.
clue_map.source: Evidence: audio waveform viewer (control room hard drives)
clue_map.reveals: The 'new' narration is synthetic/spliced — a living reader's breaths are irregular.

clue_map.clue: Room-signature analysis: the 'new' books carry a 50Hz mains hum and a specific HVAC rattle that match the OLD booth configuration.
clue_map.source: Evidence: room-tone spectrogram comparison
clue_map.reveals: Sets up an impossibility once paired with the renovation date.

clue_map.clue: Saltwire renovation invoice: new HVAC and mains rewiring of the booth completed nine weeks ago (dated, signed).
clue_map.source: Location: control room inventory binder / Evidence: contractor invoice
clue_map.reveals: PRIMARY LOCK: any genuinely new recording would carry the NEW room signature; the 'new' books carry the OLD one, so they were assembled from pre-renovation source — they cannot have been recorded by a living Della on their claimed dates. Death predates them.

clue_map.clue: A spliced sentence pronounces a word Della consistently mispronounces correctly everywhere else in her catalog.
clue_map.source: Evidence: audio comparison (archive masters vs. 'new' track)
clue_map.reveals: Charming human-detail corroboration: the model normalized her quirk — it is not her live performance.

clue_map.clue: Della's smart insulin pen dose log + an uncollected food-delivery receipt at her door timestamped that night.
clue_map.source: Evidence: insulin pen data + delivery app receipt
clue_map.reveals: She dosed normally for a meal she never reached — the mechanism of an accidental hypoglycemic collapse, not an overdose.

clue_map.clue: CGM cloud data: glucose curve a gradual five-hour downward slope to flatline eleven weeks ago (NOT a sharp injection crash).
clue_map.source: Evidence: CGM cloud account (gated — requires the phone passcode found via the audio archive)
clue_map.reveals: CONFIRMS the audio-derived death date and confirms accidental gradual hypoglycemia, not murder. Gated so it cannot shortcut the audio deduction.

clue_map.clue: Session autosave 'talkback' track: 90 seconds of Della slurring 'Theo... I can't... the light's wrong' and Theo's delayed '...you good? give me five,' then silence.
clue_map.source: Evidence: DAW session autosave (control room) / surfaced by examining timestamps
clue_map.reveals: The moral core: Theo was present and HEARD her dying and did not reach her in time. Not murder — but the human edge that explains the entire fraud as penance.

clue_map.clue: Studio access log: booth disarmed by Della's own code at her usual time; no second entry until Theo's morning badge.
clue_map.source: Location: control room access terminal / Evidence: access log
clue_map.reveals: She was alone; no one entered during the death window — forces accidental, isolated death.

clue_map.clue: Self-storage rental in Theo's late father's name; autopay begins three days after the true death date; electricity bill spikes (chest freezer load).
clue_map.source: Evidence: storage rental + utility records / Location: storage unit
clue_map.reveals: Where the body was kept — links the concealment to a physical site and the true timeline.

clue_map.clue: Chest-freezer handle and moving-dolly: only Priya's fingerprints; coat fibers matching Priya on the body bag.
clue_map.source: Location: storage unit (forensic exam) / Evidence: print + fiber report
clue_map.reveals: PHYSICAL SPLIT: Priya — not Theo — handled the body, the freezer, and the staging. Separates desecration/obstruction from fraud.

clue_map.clue: Hardware-store receipt in Priya's name/card: chest freezer + dolly straps, dated three days after the true death.
clue_map.source: Evidence: hardware-store receipt
clue_map.reveals: Puts the purchase of the concealment tools on Priya at the true death window.

clue_map.clue: Voice-model training logs and forged phone check-ins traced to Theo's login and DAW project files.
clue_map.source: Evidence: Saltwire drive logs + phone forensics
clue_map.reveals: PHYSICAL SPLIT: Theo — not Priya — built the synthetic voice and forged Della's 'alive' check-ins. He is the architect of the fraud.

clue_map.clue: Aldercrest contract, 'Voice Continuation Rider': delivery-while-alive keeps the licensing rights and retirement clause with Della's estate; death-before-delivery reverts them to Aldercrest in perpetuity.
clue_map.source: Evidence: contract folder (evidence locker)
clue_map.reveals: WHY the death date had to move and why the books had to be 'finished' — motive crystallizes around ownership of a voice, not insurance.

clue_map.clue: Priya's parking-garage ticket and toll records place her car at the storage unit, then near the studio, the night before discovery.
clue_map.source: Evidence: toll/parking records
clue_map.reveals: Identifies Priya as the body-mover and stager on the final night.

clue_map.clue: Gareth's email/calendar: the surprise memorial-premiere scheduled to force a living appearance or a death filing within 36 hours; internal note questioning whether the dailies 'sound like her.'
clue_map.source: People: Gareth Vale interview + Evidence: Aldercrest internal emails
clue_map.reveals: Gareth suspected the truth and engineered the event to trigger the reversion — fair red herring and a moral, non-criminal culpability the Lead can name.

clue_map.clue: Junie's 'pickup-line' session sheet: lines she recorded for a 'different project,' which appear in the synthetic seams; her keycard at odd hours.
clue_map.source: Evidence: session sheet + keycard log / People: Junie interview
clue_map.reveals: Explains why Junie looks complicit and clears her — she was used, not in on it.

act_structure: ACT 1 — THE WRONG KIND OF QUIET (Prologue/Tutorial): Mara catches the case at Della's memorial-premiere eve; the body looks hours-dead in the booth, but the 'new' Della on the speakers sounds wrong to her ruined ear. Teaches Evidence/People/Locations/Deduction via the scene, the ME field note, and Junie's tip. Ends on the first crack: the PMI doesn't match the scene.

ACT 2 — THE ROOM THAT DOESN'T EXIST: The investigation treats it as a fresh homicide and circles Theo. Audio forensics (metronomic breath, old room-signature vs. dated renovation) and the autopsy ice-crystal finding collide. LEAD decision point: pursue homicide-of-Theo, or chase the audio anomaly your damaged ear flagged. Ends on the reframe — the recordings were made in a room that no longer exists; Della has been dead for weeks.

ACT 3 — WHO WORE HER VOICE: The whendunit takes over. Storage unit, freezer, CGM (gated behind the audio-found passcode), the contract rider. The talkback autosave surfaces — the case's gut-punch — and the physical split (freezer prints = Priya, voice-model logs = Theo) forms. LEAD decision point: enter the talkback recording as evidence (name Theo's failure) or seal it.

ACT 4 — THE LAST CLEAN TAKE (Climax/Accusation): With the 36-hour clock expiring against the memorial-premiere and death-certificate filing, the Lead assembles the deduction board and must distinguish a murder that did not happen from a fraud that did — choosing an accusation framing (principal/accessory split, and whether to indict Gareth's silence on the record). Resolution weighs Theo's love-and-guilt, Priya's grief-curdled-to-greed, Gareth's profitable silence, and what is owed to a stolen death.

EPILOGUE — VOICE RETIRED: Branching by accusation framing and the talkback choice — the fate of the perpetual licensing rights, Della's mother's care, and whether Della's voice is finally allowed to fall silent as she asked.
act1_beat_sheet: COLD OPEN (no input): A black screen plays sixty seconds of Della Voss narrating — warm, irregular, human — over the title. The audio degrades into a too-clean, metronomic version of the same line. Subtitle: 'Eleven weeks. Nobody noticed.' Hard cut to rain on a Victorian row-house.

ARRIVAL (teaches Locations): Mara enters Saltwire's vocal booth. Free-look scene exam tutorial: the hanging mic, the pop filter, the coffee thermos, and the body of Della Voss, peaceful, posed as if she nodded off mid-take. A constable says it 'looks like she just dropped — heart, probably.' Tutorial prompt: examine three scene hotspots.

THE EAR (protagonist hook + theme): Over the studio monitors, Aldercrest's 'new' Della plays for the gathered mourners. Mara's hearing aid whines; she tilts her good ear, then her bad one. A first-person beat: the player is shown the waveform and asked 'Does this sound right to you?' — establishing that Mara/the player will be judging authenticity, and that she doesn't trust her own ear. (Two-player: the Partner's notebook opens here as an 'audio-discrepancy log.')

FIRST EVIDENCE (teaches Evidence locker): The ME hands Mara a field note. Tutorial: open the evidence locker, examine the note closely — lividity 'inconsistent with apparent scene.' A magnify/annotate interaction teaches close item examination. The constable shrugs; Mara doesn't.

FIRST INTERVIEW (teaches People): Junie Okafor, red-eyed, corners Mara: 'Can I tell you something stupid? Her breaths. In the new books. Nobody breathes like that.' Branching-dialogue tutorial: choose to press, reassure, or dismiss. Pressing unlocks the 'audio thread' marker. Junie's keycard alibi flag is planted as a fair red herring.

MEET THE PARTNERS-IN-FRAUD (planting, not revealing): Quick beats with Theo (calm, grieving, says Della was 'finally resting' — first lie, planted), and Priya on the phone being lawyerly about 'the estate.' Gareth's assistant confirms the memorial-premiere is in 36 hours — the ticking clock surfaces on the HUD.

THE BOARD (teaches Deduction): Back at the car, Mara pins her first three items to the Deduction Board: 'body posed,' 'PMI wrong,' 'breaths wrong.' Tutorial: drag two clues to form a tentative link. The game shows a link can be WRONG (it greys out if unsupported), teaching that gut isn't enough — only evidence connects.

LEAD-ONLY DECISION (teaches the asymmetric commit): The Partner can advise, but only the Lead commits Act 1's question: 'Treat this as a natural death and release the scene — or hold it as suspicious?' Holding it (the correct, harder choice) costs goodwill with the brass and starts the clock pressure. This teaches that the Lead owns consequences.

ACT-1 BUTTON (the first crack): If the Lead holds the scene, the ME calls back: the tissue readings are stranger than a heart attack — 'I want a full post-mortem.' Final shot: Mara, alone in the booth, plays the 'new' Della one more time, closes her bad ear with her hand, and we hear it the way she does — wrong. Smash to black. Title card: ACT 2 — THE ROOM THAT DOESN'T EXIST.
spine.twist_and_fairness: The mid-story reframe is forced, not asserted, by convergent independently-discoverable clues. The whodunit-to-whendunit pivot is locked by the ROOM SIGNATURE vs. RENOVATION DATE pair: the 'new' recordings carry the old booth's 50Hz hum and HVAC rattle, but a dated contractor invoice proves the booth was rewired nine weeks ago — so the 'new' books were built from pre-renovation source and cannot have been recorded by a living Della on their claimed dates. This is the primary, purely-physical lock (two waveforms plus one invoice, fully player-checkable), with metronomic breath and a normalized-mispronunciation splice as corroboration; the over-decisive CGM is deliberately GATED behind a phone passcode the player can only derive from the audio archive, so it confirms rather than hands over the death date — preserving the 'forensics of sound' as the spine. Murder is ruled OUT by discrete evidence, not by fiat: the autopsy shows no trauma/ligature/petechiae/foreign toxicology; the CGM shows a gradual five-hour hypoglycemic slope (not an injection crash); the smart insulin pen log plus an uncollected food order show an accidental dose-without-meal; and the access log shows she was alone with no second entry. Culpability is split by PHYSICAL evidence, not inferred remorse: only Priya's prints are on the freezer handle and dolly, with a hardware receipt and toll records placing her at the concealment site; only Theo's logins and DAW files built the voice model and forged the check-ins. Every load-bearing fact — PMI note, ice-crystal autopsy line, breath waveform, room-signature/renovation pair, insulin and CGM curves, access log, storage rental and utility spike, freezer prints, hardware receipt, contract rider, toll records, talkback autosave — sits in the evidence locker, a location, or an interview before the accusation. Nothing hinges on a confession or hidden information. The 'surprising yet inevitable' feeling comes from the room that doesn't exist: once you see the renovation date, the whole grieving tableau re-reads as maintenance of a fraud, and you realize the wrongness Mara's ear caught was true.
spine.ticking_clock: Aldercrest's surprise memorial-premiere is in roughly 36 hours. Gareth has engineered the event so it resolves in his favor either way: if Della is alive she must appear; if she has 'just died,' it becomes a tribute that triggers the perpetual-rights reversion. The conspirators cannot produce a living Della, so their only play is to deliver the final manuscript, get it accepted, and have her 'discovered' dead of natural causes timestamped after delivery — betting on a quiet death certificate with no full autopsy. The legal crystallization is the moment the death certificate is rubber-stamped at the apparent (staged) time and the rights paperwork posts at the launch: once those file, the reversion locks, the perpetual synthetic 'Della' becomes Aldercrest's forever against Della's written wish, and the cold-storage trail degrades (the thawed body decomposes past further dating, the freezer autopay can be cancelled, the storage unit emptied). Mara must prove the true, eleven-week-old time of death and stop the certificate from posting at the staged time before the launch — or the truth, the rights, and Della's wish become legally unrecoverable. Every act tightens the HUD clock; holding the scene in Act 1 spends political capital that makes the later hours harder.
spine.twoplayer_integration: The theme IS the mechanic: a single authentic voice versus a synthesized consensus, mirrored in 'two minds, one decision-maker.' Both players freely browse evidence, people, and scenes, but only the LEAD (Mara) commits binding decisions and the final accusation. The PARTNER keeps a literal AUDIO-DISCREPANCY LOG: the Partner can free-listen to any recording in the locker and flag what 'sounds wrong' — metronomic breaths, room-tone seams, the normalized mispronunciation — building the synthetic-detection thread, while the Lead must weigh whether to TRUST those flags, exactly echoing Mara's struggle to trust her own asymmetric, ashamed hearing. The structural temptation runs through both roles: the loud, obvious read is MURDER (accuse Theo, close it fast), and the brass reward it; only the Partner's patient, advisory listening and the Lead's restraint surface the quieter true crime. The Partner can be wrong — some flagged 'fakes' are genuine archival Della, teaching the Lead not to over-trust the second mind, just as Mara must not over-trust her ear. The binding climax forces the Lead to distinguish a murder-that-didn't-happen from a fraud-that-did, and to choose the accusation framing and whether to enter the talkback recording — a decision with real moral weight (Theo acted from love, guilt, and ruin; Priya from grief curdled to greed; Gareth from profitable silence) that ONE person must own, with the other's counsel still ringing in their ear. The 'one authentic voice deciding among many advising voices' is both the crime's subject and the table's reality.
spine.difficulty_integration: CASUAL: the Deduction Board offers soft nudges — when two related clues are both collected, a faint pulse suggests they may connect; the audio tools auto-annotate suspicious breath markers and room-tone seams; the phone-passcode gate gives a hint pointing to the master-tape label; and the final accusation accepts a correct core framing (old accidental death + concealment + staging) even if the player misassigns one minor accessory detail, with the epilogue noting what they got slightly wrong. Hint budget is generous and free. DETECTIVE (default): no auto-connections — the player forms every link manually and wrong links grey out without explanation; audio tools show raw waveforms/spectrograms that the player must read and mark themselves; the passcode must be derived from the archive unaided; the accusation requires the correct principal/accessory split (Theo = fraud/concealment, Priya = desecration/obstruction) backed by the specific physical clues, and a single mis-citation can be challenged at trial in the epilogue. Limited hints, each costing clock time. HARDCORE: no nudges, no auto-annotation, and the board hides clue-category labels so the player must recognize relevance themselves; red herrings (Junie's keycard, Gareth's motive, Theo-as-murderer) are presented with extra weight and the game will let you file a confident WRONG accusation with full consequences; the accusation is strict — every charge must be supported by the exact evidence items, the murder must be affirmatively ruled out with the autopsy+CGM+access-log triad, and Gareth's non-criminal culpability must be correctly categorized as moral, not charged, or the case is thrown out. The talkback-recording choice and accusation framing carry the heaviest epilogue divergence at this level.
spine.theme: What do we owe a voice after the person is gone — and who has the right to decide when it falls silent? The case interrogates authenticity versus fabrication (a real voice against a synthesized consensus), the modern violation of a person after death (a body warehoused, a wish overridden, a dying breath captured as a 'take'), and the difference between love that cannot let go and greed that will not. It asks whether the law has language for stealing someone's death, and it braids that question into Mara's own wound: to find the truth she must trust the damaged faculty she is ashamed of, and to honor Della she must let the voice that saved her finally stop. The emotional thesis: grief that refuses an ending becomes a kind of theft, and mercy is sometimes letting the last clean take be the last.
spine.remaining_weaknesses: 1) Replayability is still fundamentally single-truth: there is exactly one factual solution (old accidental death, Theo-fraud, Priya-staging), so the central reframe loses its punch on replay; the mitigation is moral-choice branching (accusation framing, the talkback seal/enter decision, indicting Gareth's silence) that changes epilogues and the fate of Della's voice/mother/rights, but a player chasing NEW factual mystery will not find it. 2) The eleven-week concealment of a beloved public narrator still requires the audience to accept that a reclusive booth artist with a controlling partner could be kept 'alive on paper' that long without an editor, fan, or the mother's facility noticing — defensible (forged check-ins, synthetic dailies, a mother with memory loss who cannot confirm) but it is the plot's softest plausibility seam and must be sold hard in the dialogue. 3) The voice-cloning hook, though now anchored to genuine 2024-2026 forensic techniques and to voice-estate law, still risks reading as zeitgeist-of-the-moment and could date; the timeless layer (grief, ownership of the dead, a detective who must trust her wound) is what insures it. 4) The audio-forensics reasoning (room-signature vs. renovation date, 50Hz hum) is the most cognitively demanding step for casual players; difficulty scaling and auto-annotation address it, but the studio-acoustics literacy required is higher than the medical clues. 5) The moral target is intentionally diffuse — there is no killer to hate — so a subset of players will still read the climax as 'a paperwork crime,' and the emotional success leans heavily on the talkback recording and Theo's collapse landing in performance; if those scenes underdeliver, the ending risks feeling like an attribution puzzle rather than a gut-punch. 6) Priya's culpability rests partly on the storage/freezer/toll chain being cleanly hers; a skeptical player may ask why a 'legally sophisticated' agent left prints and used her own card — answered in-world by panic, time-pressure, and hubris, but it remains a small strain on her competence characterization.

top3Developed.source: CASE: THE SALVAGE ROOM (پرونده اتاق نجات)

spine.title: CASE: THE SALVAGE ROOM (پرونده اتاق نجات)
spine.world: Present-day mid-sized rust-belt river city (a Cincinnati/Pittsburgh analog: the "Allegate" County seat on the Ohio-style Cordry River). A 100-year flood crested three weeks ago and drowned the County Regional Forensic Center's basement evidence vault, ruining roughly 40% of physical exhibits. The entire institution has spent three weeks in legitimate, court-sanctioned "disaster recovery" — re-collecting damaged evidence from secondary sources, re-papering custody chains, and racing to certify exhibits before pending trials. Three photographable real-world locations anchor the game: (1) THE FLOODED VAULT — the basement still being pumped: rows of steel shelving with a chest-high brown mud line on the brick, plastic exhibit bins with peeling waterlogged barcodes, industrial dehumidifiers and box fans roaring, sandbags, a triage whiteboard, a laminated shelf-location map taped to a wet pillar. (2) THE SALVAGE ROOM — a leased warehouse (the temporary lab): folding tables under work lights, drying racks of documents, blue nitrile gloves, sign-in clipboards, badge readers on every door, laptops running the LIMS evidence-tracking software, a wall of new sealed exhibit boxes. (3) THE FLOOD ZONE — a collapsed riverside self-storage facility where contractor Marcus Lund kept a work unit: mud-caked brick, a snapped roller door, an impounded boat trailer, the muddy riverbank, Lund's flood-recovered toolbox. Everything is depictable with real disaster/lab/warehouse stock photography and realistic portraits: lab techs, an evidence custodian, a QA director, a prosecutor, a defense investigator, a precinct commander, a National Guard logistics liaison.
spine.protagonist_stake: You play Detective Nadia Reyes, an Internal Case-Integrity auditor — a cop who polices other cops' paperwork, which has cost her every friend in the building except one. Six weeks ago her partner and mentor of nine years, Detective Tom Okafor — lead investigator on the trial-bound Marcus Lund homicide — filed a formal dissent memo arguing the prime suspect, Darnell Frye, was wrongly accused. Nadia, mid-audit and afraid of looking partisan, told Tom to "wait for the data" and didn't co-sign it. Eleven days later the flood hit. Tom went into the flooding basement vault during the mandatory evacuation and drowned. The official finding: heroic misadventure, a sentimental cop who went back for evidence. Nadia doesn't buy it — Tom never trusted the vault, never trusted its custodian, and would not have died for a bin of soggy bags. She requested THIS audit specifically: ostensibly to certify the flood-recovered exhibits as court-ready before the Frye trial, but really to find out what Tom was actually doing down there, and whether the case he died doubting is the case that killed him. Her concrete stake: clear Tom's name from the patronizing "well-meaning fool" obituary, exonerate Frye if Tom was right, and discharge her own guilt — the dissent memo she refused to sign is now Exhibit One in her own conscience.
spine.full_truth: There are two crimes, separated by motive and joined by water. CRIME ONE (the buried original): Senior Evidence Custodian Walter Saurin had for nine years run a quiet side business diverting seized firearms that were officially logged as "destroyed." He wasn't a swaggering crook — he was a meticulous, underpaid public servant who started with one "lost in the system" pistol to cover a gambling shortfall and rationalized the rest as victimless paperwork, each gun a small entry he could control. Marcus Lund, a facilities HVAC contractor doing the vault's climate-control retrofit, found Saurin's off-inventory locked shelf in Section C while running ductwork, and over weeks pieced together that the "destroyed" guns were physically present. Lund — not a hero, a man with his own money problems — first tried to leverage it (a hint of a cut), then, frightened, decided to report. Saurin killed Lund in the vault after hours with one of the very "destroyed" guns (serial recorded on his own disposal certificate), then dumped the body where it read as a botched robbery near Lund's storage unit. Tom Okafor caught the Lund case. His investigation was quietly drifting toward the lab — toward Saurin — NOT toward the man who became the defendant. Then County prosecution, hungry for a quick conviction, pivoted onto Darnell Frye, a low-level repeat offender who lived near the dump site and had no alibi. Tom filed his dissent. CRIME TWO (the cover Nadia audits): the flood drowned the vault and, with it, almost all the physical evidence in the Lund case — including the original ballistics samples and the original reference materials. Saurin realized the disaster was deliverance: in the court-authorized scramble to re-collect ruined exhibits, one more "recovered" set would vanish into the noise. He fabricated a clean evidentiary case against Frye: he logged a "re-collected" DNA profile placing Frye at the scene, generated a doctored ballistics report tying the murder bullet to a gun he claimed was Frye's, and back-dated the custody narrative — all under the legitimate banner of disaster re-collection. His fatal necessity: a legitimate re-collection must cite a surviving secondary source, and the flood had destroyed the real ones. So his fabricated exhibits cite sources that provably could not have yielded them (a buccal reference vial whose tamper seal is intact and unopened; a "duplicate" arrest-kit that the booking county's own log shows was never created; a ballistics comparison authored under the original analyst's account on a date when that analyst was on documented bereavement leave out of state). Tom, before the evacuation, had already grown certain the lab was rotten. He did NOT descend to save bins. He went down for the one thing Saurin could neither fake nor be sure he'd destroyed: physical confirmation of the Section C gun shelf — but crucially, Tom had ALREADY secured the proof of Lund's discovery (Lund's analog HVAC logbook) days earlier and mailed it to Nadia's home as insurance; he descended to photograph/secure the shelf itself before recovery teams could "salvage" it into oblivion. Saurin found him in the flooding dark, struck him (a wound the ME read as flood debris trauma), and let the rising water finish it — a homicide staged as the misadventure everyone wanted to believe. The "recovered" evidence is real evidence, manufactured by the institution's own custodian, and the disaster everyone treats as background is the actual crime scene. Frye is innocent. Tom was right. And Saurin's genius was never a clever trick — it was the institution's blind faith in a single trusted signature.

suspect_dossiers.name: Walter Saurin
suspect_dossiers.role: Senior Evidence Custodian; 31 years in the property room; the man who knows where every bin is. TRUE CULPRIT (murder of Marcus Lund, homicide of Det. Tom Okafor, fabrication of evidence).
suspect_dossiers.motive: Nine years of quietly diverting seized 'destroyed' firearms — begun to cover a private gambling debt and rationalized into a controlled, victimless-seeming routine. Lund discovered the off-book gun shelf and moved to report; Saurin killed him to protect the only thing he had left, his unblemished record. The flood handed him a way to convert a slow-burning audit risk into a closed conviction and a closed grave.
suspect_dossiers.secret: He killed Lund with an officially-destroyed vault gun, and he drowned Tom Okafor to stop him securing proof of the Section C shelf. He hides not behind cleverness but behind 31 years of being the most trusted, least-questioned man in the building — the person every cop hands evidence to without a second look. His self-justification ('I never touched the real cases, I only borrowed from the dead ones') is the tragedy of an ordinary man who decided he was the exception.
suspect_dossiers.red_herring: He is the most HELPFUL, most cooperative, most over-documented person Nadia meets — he volunteers records, anticipates her questions, 'finds' things for her. His relentless helpfulness reads as integrity and steers suspicion toward the evasive Bhandari and the abrasive Voss. He never lies outright; he curates. The fair-play tell is that everything he provides is a copy or a narrative, never an original he can't control.

suspect_dossiers.name: Dr. Priya Bhandari
suspect_dossiers.role: Lab QA / Quality Director; signs the certifications that make evidence court-admissible. RED HERRING.
suspect_dossiers.motive: Under existential pressure to certify the flood-recovered evidence in time for trials and save the lab's ASCLD accreditation — a failed audit would shutter the lab, void hundreds of pending cases, and end her career and her visa-dependent staff's jobs. She knowingly let the re-validation be rushed and under-witnessed.
suspect_dossiers.secret: She knew the re-collection was procedurally unsound and stayed silent to protect accreditation; she is guilty of a cover-up of institutional negligence, not of the frame or the killings. Months before the flood she emailed Saurin flagging that firearm-disposal certificates were being signed by him alone without the required second witness — a procedural complaint she never escalated, which she now realizes may have warned him. Her guilt is real but it is the wrong guilt; her evasiveness makes her look exactly like the forger.
suspect_dossiers.red_herring: She has signature authority to alter records, was on-site during recovery, and physically flinches whenever custody integrity comes up. A lazy player reads her shame as guilt. She is the system's conscience that chose silence — the human face of the institutional sin the story indicts.

suspect_dossiers.name: Eleanor Voss
suspect_dossiers.role: Defense Investigator for Darnell Frye; ex-forensic-tech turned defense, with a grudge against the lab that fired her. FALSE LEAD / right answer, wrong logic.
suspect_dossiers.motive: To free her client by any means — and to humiliate the lab that ended her career. She loudly insists ALL flood-recovered evidence is tainted garbage that must be thrown out wholesale.
suspect_dossiers.secret: She is CORRECT that Frye is innocent but for an unprovable wrong reason: she believes the flood randomly contaminated everything. She has no idea the evidence was deliberately manufactured. If Nadia adopts Voss's 'distrust everything' theory, the trial collapses on a technicality — Frye walks but Saurin is never caught, Tom's death stays 'misadventure,' and the real proof gets destroyed on schedule. She is the fair-play trap: the seductive shortcut that reaches the right verdict by a path that convicts no one and learns nothing.
suspect_dossiers.red_herring: Her aggression, her vendetta, her LIMS expertise, and her insistence on the conclusion the player is starting to suspect make her feel like either the culprit or the trustworthy ally. She is neither. Her hotel receipts prove she wasn't on-site; her theory is a cul-de-sac dressed as a highway.

suspect_dossiers.name: Captain Ron Mercer
suspect_dossiers.role: Precinct Commander; classified Tom's death as misadventure and is leaning on the audit to wrap clean. OBSTACLE / complicit-by-cowardice.
suspect_dossiers.motive: Institutional self-preservation: he needs the Frye trial to proceed and the scandal-prone lab to look competent. A homicide finding on Tom, or a fabrication finding on the lab, detonates both.
suspect_dossiers.secret: He suppressed Tom's dissent memo and pressured the ME toward 'misadventure' to keep the case clean — bureaucratic cowardice that incidentally shielded Saurin. He did not fabricate evidence or kill anyone. He also carries private guilt: Tom was his friend, and he buried his friend's last professional act. His stonewalling is a real obstacle and an honest misdirection — and his eventual crack (giving Nadia the suppressed memo) is an emotional turn, not just a clue drop.
suspect_dossiers.red_herring: He has the authority to classify Tom's death, suppress evidence, and pressure witnesses — a perfect institutional-villain silhouette. Players will suspect a conspiracy from the top. The truth is smaller and sadder: a tired man protecting an institution he stopped believing in.

suspect_dossiers.name: Darnell Frye
suspect_dossiers.role: The accused; a 34-year-old with two priors, framed. THE INNOCENT.
suspect_dossiers.motive: Apparent only: prior record, no alibi, lived eight blocks from the dump site.
suspect_dossiers.secret: He is genuinely innocent. His 'alibi gap' the night Lund died is real but innocent and self-incriminating: he was breaking a parole condition (across a county line visiting his estranged daughter, which violated a stay-away/curfew order) and refuses to say so because admitting it sends him back inside regardless — so he stays silent and looks guiltier. He is not a statistic; he is a man choosing prison over losing the right to see his kid. His humanity is what makes the frame monstrous rather than abstract.
suspect_dossiers.red_herring: His refusal to account for himself, his record, and the 'clean' evidence make him look guilty to everyone including, at first, the player. The discoverable truth of his innocent-but-illegal alibi is the moment the frame becomes personal.

clue_map.clue: Tom's suppressed dissent memo — arguing Frye is innocent and the Lund investigation was steered off its real lead under pressure
clue_map.source: People: Captain Mercer (gated — he denies it exists until confronted with the routing-log gap) / Evidence: the memo PDF once obtained
clue_map.reveals: Establishes Tom believed the conviction is wrong and the case was redirected; seeds distrust of the 'clean' evidence and gives Nadia her emotional through-line. Names the original analyst Tom trusted.

clue_map.clue: The laminated vault shelf-location map + the chest-high mud-line photo
clue_map.source: Locations: the Flooded Vault
clue_map.reveals: Section C (Saurin's area) housed an off-inventory locked shelf not on the official map — drawn in by hand. Also fixes which shelves were submerged. NOTE: this is no longer the linchpin; it establishes Section C exists and was Saurin's domain.

clue_map.clue: The three convicting exhibits' re-collection records each cite a SECONDARY SOURCE
clue_map.source: Evidence: Frye's DNA tube record, the ballistics report, the custody narrative — read in the Salvage Room LIMS
clue_map.reveals: Per disaster protocol, each 'recovered' exhibit must name the surviving secondary source it was re-collected from. This is the setup for the real linchpin: a legitimate re-collection's source must actually exist and be capable of yielding the exhibit.

clue_map.clue: LINCHPIN #1 — the buccal reference vial R-2231's own custody record + its physical tamper seal
clue_map.source: Evidence: reference vial R-2231 (examine physically) cross-referenced with Frye's DNA re-collection record
clue_map.reveals: Frye's 'recovered' DNA cites re-collection from reference vial R-2231 — but R-2231's tamper-evident seal is intact, its weight log unchanged, and its access log shows it was never opened during recovery. A real re-collection would have broken the seal and logged a draw. Therefore the DNA was NOT re-collected from any real source — it was fabricated. This contradiction is UNIQUE to fabrication and is NOT shared by any lawful salvage (which would show a broken seal and a draw entry).

clue_map.clue: LINCHPIN #2 — the booking county's arrest-kit creation log
clue_map.source: Locations: phone/records request to the neighboring booking county (a Location node: 'County Records Portal') + Evidence: the ballistics report's cited source
clue_map.reveals: The ballistics report claims it was re-collected/re-compared against a 'duplicate arrest-kit' on file with the booking county — but that county's own kit-creation log shows NO duplicate kit was ever made for Frye. The cited secondary source never existed. Second fabrication confirmed by a source that cannot be reverse-engineered or controlled by Saurin.

clue_map.clue: LINCHPIN #3 — the ballistics PDF metadata vs. the original analyst's verified absence
clue_map.source: Evidence: ballistics report document properties (author + creation date) cross-referenced with People: the original analyst's leave record (HR roster, obtainable via Bhandari)
clue_map.reveals: The forged report is authored under analyst J. Okeke's lab account and dated during the recovery window — but Okeke was on documented out-of-state bereavement leave (flight records / HR roster) and provably could not have authored it. Authorship is INFERRED (someone with admin access spoofed Okeke's account), not handed over. Combined with the keycard + write-session data, this narrows the spoofer.

clue_map.clue: The Salvage Room keycard log (multiple people present) + the LIMS write-session log
clue_map.source: Locations: Salvage Room badge reader export / Evidence: LIMS session metadata
clue_map.reveals: FAIR-PLAY FIX: the keycard log shows SEVERAL people on-site during the recovery window (Saurin, two techs, briefly Bhandari) — it does NOT name the culprit. Opportunity is narrowed by INTERSECTING three constraints: (a) the fabricated records were written in LIMS during a session that ran after the techs and Bhandari badged OUT (keycard), (b) that session used a workstation only the custodian's role can reach the firearms-disposal module from, and (c) a tech's testimony that Saurin sent everyone home early 'to handle the firearms re-log himself.' The player must combine, not read.

clue_map.clue: The firearms-disposal certificate list + Bhandari's flagged email
clue_map.source: Evidence: disposal certificate ledger / People: Dr. Bhandari (gated by reassuring her she's not the suspect)
clue_map.reveals: The serial number of the Lund murder weapon (from the ballistics) appears on the 'destroyed' disposal list — signed solely by Saurin, never witnessed. Bhandari's pre-flood email flagged the missing-witness policy violation but framed it as procedural, never escalated. Gives Saurin MEANS + MOTIVE for the original Lund killing, and shows the gun-theft was only a faint, deniable signal before the flood made it a buried certainty.

clue_map.clue: Lund's analog HVAC logbook — mailed to Nadia by Tom before he descended
clue_map.source: Locations: Nadia's home mailbox / desk (a Location node revealed in Act 2 when she finds Tom's package) — NOT recovered from the flood
clue_map.reveals: FAIR-PLAY FIX for gift-clue: Tom mailed Lund's original logbook to Nadia's home as insurance days before the flood, so Saurin never recovered it — explaining why it reaches the player AND why Saurin still feared the physical shelf. The logbook is in Lund's handwriting: 'extra locked shelf Sec C, not on inventory — asked W.S., told to forget it / counted 6 long-arms supposed destroyed,' dated weeks before Lund's death. Establishes Lund's motive-to-report and Saurin's motive-to-kill, and proves what Tom risked his life to corroborate.

clue_map.clue: Tom's homicide trace — the access logs of the VAULT door (not the warehouse) + a physical mark on the evidence Tom carried
clue_map.source: Locations: the Flooded Vault (basement stairwell badge reader / mechanical key log) + Evidence: Tom's recovered field camera / phone
clue_map.reveals: FAIR-PLAY FIX for the homicide hole: the basement vault stairwell has its OWN badge reader (separate from the Salvage Room). Its log — recovered from the access-control server before the data-retention wipe — shows Tom badged in during evacuation and, ninety seconds later, Saurin's badge opened the same door (he claimed he was 'evacuating the upper floors'). Tom's water-recovered field camera holds a final timestamped photo of the open Section C shelf AND a partial second figure's reflection in a steel cabinet; the ME's 'flood debris' head wound is re-read (with this context) as a strike inconsistent with floating debris. Means + motive + the two-badge vault-door log + the photo place Saurin in the vault with Tom — an examinable trace, not pure inference.

clue_map.clue: Frye's real alibi (innocent but illegal)
clue_map.source: People: Darnell Frye (gated — only opens if Nadia signals she's not building the case against him) / corroborated by Locations: a cross-county gas-station timestamp
clue_map.reveals: Frye's 'gap' is explained: he crossed the county line to see his estranged daughter, violating his parole curfew. Innocent of the murder, guilty of a parole technicality he'll go back inside for. Confirms the frame targeted a man whose own fear of the system made him the perfect, silent scapegoat — and makes the exoneration personal.

clue_map.clue: Eleanor Voss's 'everything is tainted' theory + her out-of-county hotel receipts
clue_map.source: People: Eleanor Voss / Evidence: her hotel folio
clue_map.reveals: Voss is provably off-site (clears her) and is RIGHT that Frye is innocent but for the wrong, unprovable reason (random flood contamination). Following her wholesale-distrust path frees Frye on a technicality but never catches Saurin and never proves Tom's homicide — the fair-play trap of a correct conclusion reached by lazy, unprovable logic.

act_structure: ACT 1 — THE COMMISSION (Prologue / tutorial): Nadia arrives to certify the flood-recovered Frye exhibits before a 72-hour trial. She tours the three locations, meets the cast, and is set up to BELIEVE her job is to defend the recovered evidence against Voss's 'flood tainted everything' attack. Teaches Evidence/People/Locations/Deduction mechanics. Ends on the first crack: the convicting exhibits each cite a secondary source — and one source's seal is intact.

ACT 2 — THE REFRAME: Pursuing 'are these sources real?', Nadia uncovers Linchpin #1 (sealed vial), #2 (nonexistent arrest-kit), #3 (spoofed analyst). The allegiance flips: she is not defending the evidence, she is auditing a fabrication. Tom's package arrives (logbook). The crime category reveals itself: someone laundered a frame inside the disaster paperwork. LEAD DECISION: which exhibits to certify vs. quarantine (binding; changes what survives the destruction clock).

ACT 3 — THE TWO MINDS: Narrowing WHO could fabricate. Keycard + LIMS write-session + tech testimony intersect on the custodian. Bhandari (cleared, negligence not frame) and Mercer (cracks, hands over the memo) are resolved as red herrings; Voss is exposed as the right-answer/wrong-logic trap. The disposal list ties the murder weapon to Saurin → original Lund motive. LEAD DECISION: pursue the institutional-negligence angle (safe, accreditation scandal, Frye walks on technicality) vs. the homicide-fabrication angle (dangerous, indicts a trusted colleague, requires the vault-door proof).

ACT 4 — THE VAULT: Racing the data-wipe and biohazard-destruction clock, Nadia recovers the vault stairwell two-badge log and Tom's field camera, re-reads the ME finding, and confronts Saurin — whose calm, ordinary self-justification ('I only borrowed from the dead cases') lands as the human tragedy the case was missing. FINAL ACCUSATION: name Saurin for the murder of Marcus Lund, the homicide of Det. Tom Okafor, and the fabrication of evidence — Frye exonerated. Moral coda: the institution's single trusted signature, the thing Nadia herself failed to challenge when she wouldn't co-sign Tom's memo, is what the whole case indicts.
act1_beat_sheet: COLD OPEN (cinematic, non-interactive): Six weeks ago, in rain. Tom Okafor on Nadia's doorstep, soaked, pressing a manila envelope into her hands: 'If the data's clean, I'm wrong and you can laugh at me. If it's not, you'll know what to do.' Nadia, tired, tells him to wait for the audit and not to file the dissent yet. He files it anyway. Hard cut to a TV news chyron: 'VETERAN DETECTIVE DROWNS IN FLOODED EVIDENCE VAULT — CALLED HERO.' Nadia, present day, staring at the unopened-looking envelope she never thought about again. This plants the logbook payoff and her guilt without explaining either.

BEAT 1 — ARRIVAL / FRAMING THE WRONG JOB (teaches Locations): Nadia badges into the Salvage Room warehouse. Captain Mercer greets her warmly and frames her mission: 'Defense is going to scream the flood tainted everything. Certify the Frye exhibits, shut Voss up, let the trial run.' Tutorial: the player learns to examine a LOCATION (pan the warehouse, click the badge reader, the drying racks, the LIMS laptop). The game tells the player their goal is to DEFEND the evidence — the deliberate misframe the reframe will overturn.

BEAT 2 — MEET THE CUSTODIAN (teaches People + branching dialogue): Walter Saurin, warm and grandfatherly, gives Nadia the grand tour and proactively hands her a tidy binder: 'Everything's re-papered, every exhibit, every source. Thirty-one years, never lost a bag.' Tutorial: branching dialogue, and the concept of SECRETS gated behind trust/evidence. Saurin volunteers so much that the player is taught to feel reassured — establishing him as the helpful red herring. One throwaway line ('the firearms re-log I handled myself, didn't want the kids near live serials') is the buried Act-3 payoff.

BEAT 3 — THE EVIDENCE LOCKER (teaches Evidence + forensic notes): Nadia opens the case board's Evidence locker. Tutorial: examine the three convicting exhibits (DNA tube, ballistics report, custody card), read FORENSIC NOTES, and inspect document METADATA on the ballistics PDF. The notes establish the disaster context: '~40% of exhibits ruined; this exhibit RE-COLLECTED from secondary source per protocol.' The player is taught that dry/clean is NORMAL — pre-empting and defusing the old flawed linchpin so the real one will land.

BEAT 4 — THE FLOODED VAULT (teaches Locations examination + the mud line): Descent into the still-draining basement. Tutorial: environmental examination — click the dehumidifiers, the triage whiteboard, the chest-high mud line, and the laminated shelf map. Discovery: Section C has a hand-drawn locked shelf NOT on the printed inventory. The player notes 'off-inventory shelf in the custodian's section' without yet knowing its meaning. Atmosphere: this is where Tom died; a small evidence-tech memorial photo is taped to a pillar, twisting the knife.

BEAT 5 — VOSS AMBUSH (teaches the trap archetype): Eleanor Voss intercepts Nadia outside: loud, abrasive, certain — 'Every bag in that building is poison. Throw it ALL out. Your partner knew it.' Tutorial: dialogue where a character pushes a SHORTCUT conclusion. The player is invited to either nod along (seeding the trap) or push back. Voss name-drops that Tom was 'asking about sources, not water damage' — a fair-play breadcrumb that the real question is provenance, not contamination.

BEAT 6 — THE DEDUCTION BOARD (teaches the core mechanic): Back at the board, the game prompts the first connection. Nadia has two cards: 'Convicting exhibits RE-COLLECTED from secondary sources' (Evidence) and 'Disaster protocol: re-collection must cite a real surviving source' (forensic note). Tutorial: drag-to-connect two clues to form a CONCLUSION card: 'Then I can verify each exhibit by checking its cited source still exists and could yield it.' This teaches the deduction loop AND hands the player the method that breaks the whole case.

BEAT 7 — THE FIRST CRACK / ACT-OUT HOOK (the hook): Following her own conclusion, Nadia physically examines the cited source for the DNA — reference vial R-2231 — and finds its tamper seal INTACT, weight unchanged, never logged open. Beat of dawning unease: a real re-collection breaks the seal. The exhibit that convicts Frye points to a source that was never touched. Final tutorial prompt: form the conclusion card 'This exhibit could not have been re-collected — so where did it come from?' SMASH to Act 1 title card / first LEAD checkpoint. The player now WANTS the reframe; the hook is the wrongness of clean paper over an unopened seal.

BEAT 8 — TWO-PLAYER ONBOARDING (woven through, not a separate beat): From Beat 3 on, the PARTNER is taught to keep a SUSPICIONS NOTEBOOK (free-roam, flag any physical-vs-paper mismatch) while the LEAD is taught that only they can COMMIT a conclusion to the board.
